summary: The anterior compartment of the arm contains biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis.
The anterior compartment of the arm contains biceps brachii, triceps, and brachioradialis.
The anterior compartment of the arm contains only biceps brachii and triceps.
Saturday night paralysis involves compression of the radial nerve in the spiral groove of the humerus.
Saturday night paralysis involves compression of the ulnar nerve at the elbow.
Saturday night paralysis involves compression of the median nerve in the carpal tunnel.
The cubital fossa is bounded laterally by brachioradialis and medially by pronator teres.
The cubital fossa is bounded laterally by pronator teres and medially by brachioradialis.
The cubital fossa is bounded laterally by biceps brachii and medially by triceps.
The median cubital vein connects the cephalic and basilic veins and is the preferred venipuncture site.
The median cubital vein connects the brachial and radial veins.
The basilic vein is the preferred site for venipuncture because it is most superficial.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies all three muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm.
The median nerve supplies all three muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm.
The radial nerve supplies all three muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm.
The brachial artery divides at the cubital fossa into the radial and ulnar arteries.
The brachial artery divides at the axilla into the radial and ulnar arteries.
The brachial artery continues as the radial artery without dividing.
Radial nerve injury causes wrist drop due to paralysis of wrist and finger extensors.
Radial nerve injury causes wrist drop due to paralysis of wrist and finger flexors.
Radial nerve injury causes claw hand due to paralysis of the interossei.
